summary:
Francisco Pizarro's conquest of the Inca Empire (1532–1533 AD) was arguably the most improbable military victory in history — 168 Spanish soldiers with horses and firearms capturing the world's largest empire, with a population of perhaps 10-12 million people and armies of hundreds of thousands, through a combination of audacity, disease (smallpox had already devastated the Inca population before Pizarro arrived), and exploitation of the civil war between two claimants to the throne. Pizarro had led two earlier failed expeditions to South America before his third, royal-backed voyage of 1531; landing in Ecuador with 168 men, 67 horses, and 4 cannon, he marched into the highlands and secured a meeting with the Inca emperor Atahualpa at Cajamarca.

The confrontation at Cajamarca (November 15, 1532) was the decisive moment: Pizarro's ambush of Atahualpa's escort — surprising an encampment of thousands with disciplined cavalry and gunfire — killed several thousand Inca attendants without a single Spanish death, capturing Atahualpa in the chaos. The empire's paralysis thereafter reflected its extreme centralization: with the god-emperor captured, the administrative machine could not function autonomously. Atahualpa's ransom — filling a large room with gold and two more with silver — was accepted and paid, yet Pizarro had him strangled in 1533 nonetheless, fearing his influence. The subsequent Spanish march to Cusco and installation of puppet Inca rulers completed the conquest of the empire's heartland, though Inca resistance continued for decades under Neo-Inca states in the highlands.
